chemicals.name: Based on the provided sources, the primary substance of concern found in chewing gum is the artificial sweetener xylitol. However

chemicals.concentration_notes: or potency: A single stick of sugar-free gum can contain up to 1 gram of xylitol, which is enough to cause severe poisoning in a small dog10. By contrast, experimental studies have administered massive doses of up to 1,000 mg/kg (1 gram per kilogram of body weight) to cats without inducing any toxic effects or clinical signs of illness25. Despite cats' apparent immunity, veterinarians still strong
chemicals.description: in cats: In dogs, xylitol is exquisitely toxic; it tricks the pancreas into releasing a massive surge of insulin, which rapidly drives down blood sugar (severe hypoglycemia) and can lead to acute liver failure and death2.... However, cats appear to be naturally resistant to xylitol toxicity2. Research—including a 2018 experimental study—has demonstrated that the feline pancreas does not respond to
chemicals.name: Xylitol (Artificial Sweetener)

chemicals.concentration_notes: or potency: Even a single swallowed piece of gum can pose a choking hazard or cause a gastrointestinal blockage in a small animal like a cat
chemicals.description: in cats: While standard sugary gum or the gum base itself is not chemically toxic, it presents a physical danger. Swallowed chewing gum cannot be broken down by the feline digestive system and can act as a foreign body, potentially causing a dangerous obstruction or blockage in the esophagus, stomach, or intestines11. chemicals.name: Indigestible Gum Base (Physical Hazard)

description: Chewing gum is potentially toxic to cats depending on its ingredients. Sugar-free gum and many mints contain xylitol — an artificial sweetener that is highly toxic to cats and dogs. Xylitol triggers a massive release of insulin from the pancreas, causing dangerous hypoglycaemia, and at higher doses causes liver failure. Regular sugary gum is not chemically toxic but poses a physical hazard.
emergencyNote: While experimental studies and poison control records indicate that cats appear to be naturally resistant to xylitol
name: Gum
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: xylitol (in sugar-free gum, some mints, and dental products) — check ingredient labels
Mechanism: xylitol stimulates insulin secretion independent of blood glucose levels, causing severe hypoglycaemia; hepatotoxic at higher doses
Symptoms: vomiting, weakness, incoordination (ataxia), tremors, seizures (from low blood sugar); liver failure may follow 24–72 hours later
Regular sugar-based gum: choking hazard and GI obstruction risk from swallowed gum mass; not chemically toxic
Keep all gum, breath mints, and xylitol-containing products sealed and stored away from cats; any suspected xylitol ingestion is an emergency

symptoms.name: Unlike dogs, who suffer severe and life-threatening reactions to chewing gum containing the artificial sweetener xylitol
Transient Salivation (Drooling)
symptoms.notes: In a 2018 experimental study where cats were given highly toxic oral doses of xylitol (up to 1,000 mg/kg), the cats exhibited no changes in their blood glucose, liver enzymes, or hematological parameters. symptoms.name: Gastrointestinal Upset
symptoms.notes: Very high levels of consumption of artificial sweeteners and sugar substitutes can trigger general stomach upset and gastrointestinal distress in cats7. symptoms.name: Hypoglycemia and Liver Failure (Theoretical / Rare)
symptoms.notes: While scientific studies and major poison control centers report that cats do not react to xylitol with the dangerous insulin surge seen in dogs2..., some veterinary sources still urge extreme caution. Because individual cats may have atypical metabolisms, it is theoretically suggested that xylitol

treatments.name: While experimental studies and poison control records indicate that cats appear to be naturally resistant to xylitol

treatments.description: of the procedure: If the gum was ingested very recently, the veterinarian will administer a specific injection to make the cat vomit, emptying the stomach to minimize the absorption of any potential toxins7. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Early Gastrointestinal Decontamination
treatments.notes: This procedure is only helpful if performed very quickly after ingestion; it is too late to induce vomiting if symptoms have already developed7. Pet owners should never attempt to induce vomiting at home, as there are no safe methods for doing so in cats without professional veterinary care

treatments.description: of the procedure: If the cat shows symptoms of low blood sugar, they will be admitted to the veterinary clinic as an in-patient for intensive monitoring. The veterinary team will administer fluids containing sugar (dextrose) directly into the cat's veins to stabilize blood glucose levels8. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Hypoglycemia Management (If Symptoms Develop)
treatments.notes: While waiting for veterinary care, if a cat begins to show symptoms of low blood sugar (such as unsteadiness or tremors), owners can provide emergency home first aid by smearing honey, jam, or sugary water on the cat's gums, especially if swallowing is risky

treatments.description: of the procedure: The veterinarian will run blood tests to confirm blood glucose levels and perform urinalysis. These tests help identify any instances of internal organ damage (such as liver impairment) and guide the ongoing treatment strategy610. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Diagnostic Testing and Ongoing Supportive Care
treatments.notes: In experimental studies where cats were given massive doses of xylitol (up to 1,000 mg/kg), their hematological and biochemical parameters remained unchanged111.
